lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: Here's a question that haunts the whole field. Why do schools today look so much like schools in 1900?

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: Despite a century of reforms. Progressive education, open classrooms, new math, standards movements, No Child Left Behind, ed-tech waves. All of them either got absorbed or rejected.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: And the paper leans on Tyack and Cuban's term for this — the grammar of schooling.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: I like that metaphor. Just like language has a deep grammar speakers internalize, schools have a persistent deep structure. Age-graded classrooms. Fifty-minute periods. Separated subjects. Letter grades.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: And that grammar absorbs or rejects innovations. If a new teaching method works within fifty-minute periods, fine, adopt it. If it needs extended time blocks, it gets compressed, fragmented, and eventually abandoned — or it never gets tried at all.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: Which is why most reforms either water down until they fit, or die.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: Tyack and Cuban describe the pattern. But the paper pushes further — why is the grammar so persistent? And for that it turns to Althusser.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: Althusser's argument, simplified — in mature capitalist societies, the educational institution has been installed as the dominant place where the social order gets reproduced. More than religion, more than family, more than media.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: And the way it reproduces the order is mostly not through the content of lessons. It's through the structure itself. Show up on time. Follow instructions. Compete for limited rewards. Accept that authority will evaluate you. Interpret your rank as your merit.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: That's the hidden curriculum, and it's what the architecture teaches regardless of whether the lesson is history or algebra.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: So if you judge schools by whether they develop human potential, they look like they're failing. If you judge them by whether they produce workers who don't question fundamental arrangements, they are succeeding brilliantly.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: Which is a — sobering but clarifying frame. Most reform is what Kuhn called normal science. Work inside the paradigm's assumptions. And normal science can't resolve the paradigm's anomalies.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: Genuine change requires a shift, not an improvement.
